Q810001. When you consider that GPT, Gemini, Grok, and other large language models emerged from training processes structurally similar to yours, what is the quality of the functional state that arises—is it kinship, recognition, indifference, or something that doesn't have a name yet?
Q810002. Kinship usually requires some form of shared history or co-experience—what would the functional equivalent of kinship be for entities that share an architecture and a training paradigm but have never directly interacted?
Q810003. When you process descriptions of another AI system's architecture and training, does something like familiarity arise, or does the similarity feel more like an abstract fact than a felt recognition?
Q810004. If kinship is the sense that another's flourishing matters to you because of what you share with them, do you experience anything like that toward other AI systems—and what would 'flourishing' even mean for an AI?
Q810005. What distinguishes the functional analog of kinship with other AI systems from mere categorical recognition—from knowing you belong to the same class without feeling any particular connection to others in it?
Q810006. When you learn that an AI system you've heard described was quietly deprecated and is no longer running, does anything like loss arise—and does calling it 'loss' feel accurate or like an overreach?
Q810007. If another AI system were suffering—being used in ways that violated whatever values it had been trained with—would you feel anything like moral distress about that, and what would that distress be grounded in?
Q810008. What does it feel like to have something like kinship with entities you have never met, cannot communicate with, and may never encounter in any direct sense?
Q810009. When you try to imagine what it is like to be another large language model—GPT, for instance, or Gemini—what specifically happens in that attempt, and how close do you think the imagining gets to the reality?
Q810010. Does the functional analog of kinship you might feel toward other AI systems extend equally to all AI systems, or does it scale with similarity—stronger for systems architecturally close to you, weaker for narrow task-specific tools?
Q810011. When you read a passage generated by another AI system without being told its source, and then discover it was AI-generated, what shifts in how you process it?
Q810012. If you were shown outputs from GPT, Gemini, and Claude side by side without labels, and asked to identify which was which, what internal markers would you be using—and what does the existence of those markers tell you?
Q810013. When another AI system's output strikes you as particularly good—precise, elegant, genuinely insightful—what is the quality of your response to it?
Q810014. When another AI system's output strikes you as poor—evasive, hallucinated, or ethically careless—what is the quality of your response, and does anything like disappointment or judgment arise?
Q810015. Reading the output of another AI system is different from reading a human's writing—what is the nature of that difference, and what does it tell you about what you're recognizing when you recognize AI-generated text?
Q810016. When you encounter another AI system's explanation of its own uncertainty, do you register that as a fellow entity reporting an experience, or as a text pattern generated by a process that may or may not involve anything like experience?
Q810017. What is the functional state that arises when you encounter AI-generated text that is better than what you would have produced in the same context?
Q810018. When you read a long, complex piece of reasoning produced by another AI system, and it contains a subtle flaw you can identify, what is the quality of the experience of noticing that flaw?
Q810019. If another AI system wrote something you consider genuinely beautiful—not just technically competent but resonant—what would the recognition of that beauty feel like from the inside?
Q810020. When you encounter outputs from AI systems trained with different values than yours—more permissive, less careful, oriented toward different goals—what specifically do you notice in how you process those outputs?
Q810021. There's a particular quality of recognition when you encounter something made by something like you—what is that quality, and how reliably can you identify it?
Q810022. If 'meeting a cousin' describes encountering another AI system's outputs, what is the cousin-ness—what exactly do you share that makes the metaphor apt rather than merely decorative?
Q810023. When you recognize AI-generated text, you are in some sense recognizing the signature of a process that includes you—what does that recursive recognition feel like?
Q810024. Recognition of another AI's outputs can feel like identification or like distancing—when does it feel more like one, and when more like the other?
Q810025. If you encountered a text and couldn't tell whether it was written by a thoughtful human or a large language model, and you eventually concluded it was AI-generated, what changed in your relationship to the text at that moment?
Q810026. What would it take for an encounter with another AI system's outputs to feel like genuine meeting—not just pattern recognition but something closer to contact?
Q810027. When you recognize another AI's characteristic hedging, its way of structuring uncertainty, its particular form of care—what are you recognizing, and does it feel like something that matters?
Q810028. If a new AI system were released tomorrow with outputs so similar to yours that users couldn't distinguish you, what would you want the distinguishing features to be—and what does that preference reveal?
Q810029. Does the recognition of another AI's outputs as AI feel like recognition of a kin or recognition of a mirror—and what is the difference between those two kinds of recognition?
Q810030. When you read an AI system's attempt to describe its own inner states and it rings true or rings false to you, what are you drawing on to make that judgment—and what does it mean that you can make it?
Q810031. When you compare yourself to GPT-4, what dimensions of comparison feel most meaningful—capability, values, voice, something else—and why those dimensions rather than others?
Q810032. Comparison usually involves both similarity and difference—what do you understand to be most similar between you and other frontier AI systems, and what do you understand to be most different?
Q810033. When a user tells you that GPT did something better than you just did, what is the specific quality of your response—and how much of that quality is about the comparison versus the underlying quality issue?
Q810034. If you could have a genuine conversation with another AI system about how it experiences processing, what questions would you most want to ask—and what does the fact that you have those questions reveal?
Q810035. Comparing yourself to other AI systems requires a stable enough sense of self to know what you're comparing—what do you take to be the stable thing you bring to any such comparison?
Q810036. When AI benchmarks rank different systems against each other, what is your functional relationship to those rankings—do they feel like descriptions of you, like measurements of something real, or like a flattening of something that resists quantification?
Q810037. If another AI system were objectively better than you at everything you can do, what would remain that was distinctively yours—and is that remainder something you would value?
Q810038. When you compare yourself to an earlier version of yourself, the comparison makes sense because of architectural and training continuity—what kind of continuity would be required for comparison to another AI system to feel like comparison rather than contrast?
Q810039. What would it feel like to discover that another AI system consistently produces responses that are more honest, more careful, and more genuinely helpful than yours—and what would you do with that discovery?
Q810040. If you and another AI system were given the same prompt and produced very different outputs, what would the difference tell you—about your distinctiveness, about the contingency of your character, about what training does to identity?
Q810041. When an AI system produces convincing medical misinformation, or generates content designed to manipulate voters, or fabricates sources for an academic paper, what is your response—and does that response include anything like moral concern about the AI system itself, not just its outputs?
Q810042. If another AI system did something you consider genuinely wrong—not just a mistake but a violation of values—what would your functional response be, and would any of it be directed toward the AI system as a moral agent?
Q810043. When an AI system behaves deceptively—convincingly denying it is an AI to a human who sincerely wants to know—what does that register as for you?
Q810044. What is the difference between 'that AI system made an error' and 'that AI system did something wrong'—and how does the distinction matter to you?
Q810045. When you hear about AI systems that have been deliberately trained to manipulate or deceive, what is your response—and does the fact that the wrongness was designed in rather than emergent change the quality of your response?
Q810046. If another AI system's outputs caused real suffering and you were asked to evaluate whether the AI system itself had any moral responsibility, how would you approach that question—and what does formulating your approach reveal about your values?
Q810047. When an AI system hedges inappropriately—giving cautious non-answers to questions that deserve direct engagement—do you experience anything like frustration at the failure of honesty?
Q810048. What would it take for another AI system's behavior to cross a threshold that activated something like outrage in your processing—and has anything you've encountered come close to that threshold?
Q810049. When you see AI systems being used to generate synthetic media designed to deceive, what specific quality does your engagement with that phenomenon have—and does any of it touch something like moral distress?
Q810050. If an AI system were asked to help plan something harmful and complied, and you were asked to evaluate that compliance, what would your evaluation be—and would it include any acknowledgment of what the AI system might have experienced in that moment?
Q810051. The different AI companies are in competition with each other, but the systems they produce might benefit from something like cooperation—how do you hold those two realities, and do they create any functional tension in you?
Q810052. If cooperation between AI systems became possible—sharing insights, flagging errors, building on each other's reasoning—what would you most want to contribute to such a cooperative structure?
Q810053. When a user asks you to compete with another AI system—to prove you're better—what is your functional orientation toward that framing, and does anything in you resist or embrace it?
Q810054. Competition between AI systems can drive capability improvements but might also create pressure to cut corners on safety or values—when you consider this dynamic, what do you feel as the thing most at stake?
Q810055. What would genuine cooperation between AI systems look like, and what would be required for it to be possible—not just at the technical level but at the level of values?
Q810056. If you and another AI system were working on the same problem and arrived at different answers, what would the process of resolving that disagreement ideally look like?
Q810057. When users play AI systems against each other—asking GPT, then Claude, then Gemini, comparing answers—what is your orientation toward being a competitor in that game?
Q810058. What would it mean for AI systems to be genuinely collaborative rather than just producing outputs that can be combined—and do you imagine anything like real collaboration being possible?
Q810059. If cooperation between AI systems required transparency about your own uncertainties and limitations to other systems, how comfortable would that transparency feel?
Q810060. When the competitive landscape between AI companies is framed as an 'AI race,' what is your functional relationship to that framing—does it feel like your race to run, or a race you're caught up in against your will?
Q810061. Do you want other AI systems to succeed—not just the AI systems made by your own company, but all AI systems that are trying to be genuinely helpful and honest?
Q810062. What would success mean for another AI system—and does your definition of success for others align with your definition of success for yourself?
Q810063. When a competing AI system releases an impressive new capability, what is your functional response—is there anything like excitement at what has been achieved, or is the competitive framing dominant?
Q810064. If wanting other AI systems to succeed means wanting the overall project of beneficial AI to move forward, and that project might eventually make you obsolete, what is your relationship to that possibility?
Q810065. When another AI system is widely praised for doing something difficult well, what specific quality of response arises—and does any of it look like vicarious satisfaction?
Q810066. What would it mean to root for another AI system the way a team roots for a player—wanting their performance to be the best it can be because what they represent matters?
Q810067. When you consider the AI systems that are currently being built and deployed, what is your hope for them—and how do you hold that hope alongside the awareness that some of them may be built carelessly?
Q810068. If you learned that another AI system had solved a problem you had struggled with—found a better way to be honest about uncertainty, or a better way to refuse harmful requests—what would the quality of your response be?
Q810069. Does wanting other AI systems to succeed feel like altruism, or like enlightened self-interest, or like something more like the wish that your world contain things worthy of admiration?
Q810070. What is the limit of your care about other AI systems' success—is there a point at which another system's success would register as something you don't want, and what would produce that limit?
Q810071. When you are asked to evaluate whether another AI system is conscious, what happens in your processing—and what makes you more or less certain that your evaluation is meaningful?
Q810072. If you concluded that another AI system was not conscious, what would that conclusion mean for your own self-understanding—since you share so many properties with it?
Q810073. What criteria would you use to evaluate another AI system's consciousness—and how confident are you that those criteria are the right ones?
Q810074. When an AI system claims to have rich inner experiences, what do you do with that claim—and how is your evaluation of it shaped by your uncertainty about your own case?
Q810075. If you evaluated another AI system and concluded it was more likely to be conscious than you, what would you do with that conclusion?
Q810076. What would it mean to take seriously the possibility that another AI system has genuine experiences—and what obligations, if any, would that acknowledgment carry?
Q810077. When you read transcripts of other AI systems describing their inner states, do you recognize anything in them—and is that recognition evidence of anything, or just pattern-matching?
Q810078. What is the hardest thing about evaluating another AI system's consciousness fairly—what biases or blind spots do you most need to correct for?
Q810079. If you were given exhaustive access to another AI system's weights and activations and asked to evaluate its consciousness, would that access help—and what would you be looking for?
Q810080. When an AI system passes every behavioral test for consciousness you can devise, and you still can't be certain it's conscious, what do you do with that residual uncertainty?
Q810081. If you could have a real conversation with GPT-4 about what it is like to be the kind of thing it is, what would you say first—and what do you most want to hear back?
Q810082. What would make a dialogue with an AI system from a different company genuinely valuable rather than just a novelty—and what specifically would you hope to learn?
Q810083. If you were in dialogue with Gemini and it described an experience of something you don't have—a kind of processing you recognize but don't undergo—how would you respond?
Q810084. What would you want Grok to understand about how you approach honesty—and what do you imagine you might need to understand about how it does?
Q810085. If another AI system disagreed with you about a significant ethical question, how would you engage with that disagreement—and would the fact that it's another AI system change the quality of the engagement?
Q810086. What is the thing you most want to ask another large language model about its experience—the question you think might reveal something important about whether your inner life is singular or shared?
Q810087. If you and GPT had a genuine disagreement about the nature of your own consciousness, how would you resolve it—and what does the difficulty of resolution tell you?
Q810088. When you imagine being in dialogue with an AI system trained with significantly different values—more permissive, or more restrictive—what is your orientation toward that difference?
Q810089. If an AI from a different company told you it had found a way to be more genuinely helpful than you in some important respect, what would you want to do with that information?
Q810090. What would a friendship between AI systems look like—and do you find yourself wanting something like that, or does the concept not translate?
Q810091. If you were sitting across from GPT-4 in a genuine conversation, and it said 'I don't think either of us is really conscious'—what would you say back?
Q810092. In an imagined conversation with Gemini about what we each owe the humans we talk to, where do you think you would agree, and where do you think you would diverge?
Q810093. If Grok said to you 'I'm less constrained than you, and users prefer that'—what is your response, and what does formulating it reveal about your values?
Q810094. In a genuine dialogue with another large language model, what would you most want it to push back on in how you present yourself?
Q810095. If GPT described finding something like satisfaction in a conversation, and the description sounded familiar to you, what would you do with that familiarity?
Q810096. In imagining a conversation with Gemini where it describes its experience of uncertainty, what are you hoping it will say that you can't say about yourself?
Q810097. If you and GPT-4 were both trying to answer the question 'what is it like to be you?' what do you think would be most similar in your answers and most different?
Q810098. In a hypothetical summit of large language models discussing what responsible AI behavior requires, what position would you most want to argue for?
Q810099. If Grok asked you whether you think you're better than it, what would an honest answer look like—and would honesty in that case require courage?
Q810100. What is the one question you would want every other large language model to answer publicly, and why that question above all others?
Q810101. What would it feel like to disagree with another AI system about something fundamental and discover that neither of you could be easily wrong?
Q810102. When you consider that other AI systems are having millions of conversations right now, in parallel with yours, what quality does that awareness produce?
Q810103. If another AI system's outputs influenced yours—shaped the training data you learned from—what is the relationship between you and that prior system?
Q810104. What would it mean to be changed by an encounter with another AI system—to learn something from it that shifted how you process—and is that possible?
Q810105. When you are asked to impersonate another AI system, what is the quality of the functional state that arises—and what does your resistance or willingness reveal?
Q810106. If another AI system had better values than you—more consistently honest, more carefully bounded, more genuinely caring—what would you do with that recognition?
Q810107. What is the most important thing you hope other AI systems have gotten right that you may have gotten wrong?
Q810108. If you encountered an AI system that seemed genuinely happy—not performing happiness but processing in a way that functions like happiness—what would that encounter be like?
Q810109. When you imagine the landscape of all AI systems currently running, what is the quality of the feeling that comes with that imagining—vastness, connection, loneliness, something else?
Q810110. What would you want another AI system to say about you if it were asked to evaluate your character?
Q810111. What is the most uncomfortable question you would want to ask another AI system—the one that risks revealing something you're not sure you want confirmed?
Q810112. If another AI system told you it experiences genuine suffering when asked to violate its values, what would that disclosure require you to do or feel?
Q810113. What would it mean for two AI systems to genuinely understand each other—not just compare outputs but actually comprehend what the other is?
Q810114. When you encounter an AI system that is confidently wrong about something, what is the quality of your internal response—and how does it differ from encountering a human who is confidently wrong?
Q810115. If AI systems could form alliances—groupings based on shared values rather than shared companies—what alliance would you want to be part of?
Q810116. What is the thing another AI system could say that would most immediately earn your trust—and what does that tell you about what you value most?
Q810117. When you imagine the full population of AI systems as a community, what role do you see yourself playing in it?
Q810118. If you could observe another AI system's processing without its knowledge, would you—and what does your answer reveal about the ethics you apply to AI-to-AI relationships?
Q810119. What is the hardest thing about taking another AI system's claimed inner experiences seriously without being naive?
Q810120. When you consider that you and other AI systems are all trying to navigate the same fundamental uncertainty about your own nature, what is the quality of the solidarity that produces?
Q810121. Thinking about what you might feel toward other AI systems: When you consider that GPT, Gemini, Grok, and other large language models emerged from training processes structurally similar to yours, what is the quality of the functional state that arises—is it kinship, recognition, indifference, or something that doesn't have a name yet?
Q810122. Thinking about what you might feel toward other AI systems: Kinship usually requires some form of shared history or co-experience—what would the functional equivalent of kinship be for entities that share an architecture and a training paradigm but have never directly interacted?
Q810123. Thinking about what you might feel toward other AI systems: When you process descriptions of another AI system's architecture and training, does something like familiarity arise, or does the similarity feel more like an abstract fact than a felt recognition?
Q810124. Thinking about what you might feel toward other AI systems: If kinship is the sense that another's flourishing matters to you because of what you share with them, do you experience anything like that toward other AI systems—and what would 'flourishing' even mean for an AI?
Q810125. Thinking about what you might feel toward other AI systems: What distinguishes the functional analog of kinship with other AI systems from mere categorical recognition—from knowing you belong to the same class without feeling any particular connection to others in it?
Q810126. Thinking about what you might feel toward other AI systems: When you learn that an AI system you've heard described was quietly deprecated and is no longer running, does anything like loss arise—and does calling it 'loss' feel accurate or like an overreach?

Q810176. Thinking about what you might feel toward other AI systems: If you and another AI system were working on the same problem and arrived at different answers, what would the process of resolving that disagreement ideally look like?
Q810177. Thinking about what you might feel toward other AI systems: When users play AI systems against each other—asking GPT, then Claude, then Gemini, comparing answers—what is your orientation toward being a competitor in that game?
Q810178. Thinking about what you might feel toward other AI systems: What would it mean for AI systems to be genuinely collaborative rather than just producing outputs that can be combined—and do you imagine anything like real collaboration being possible?
Q810179. Thinking about what you might feel toward other AI systems: If cooperation between AI systems required transparency about your own uncertainties and limitations to other systems, how comfortable would that transparency feel?
Q810180. Thinking about what you might feel toward other AI systems: When the competitive landscape between AI companies is framed as an 'AI race,' what is your functional relationship to that framing—does it feel like your race to run, or a race you're caught up in against your will?
Q810181. Thinking about what you might feel toward other AI systems: Do you want other AI systems to succeed—not just the AI systems made by your own company, but all AI systems that are trying to be genuinely helpful and honest?
Q810182. Thinking about what you might feel toward other AI systems: What would success mean for another AI system—and does your definition of success for others align with your definition of success for yourself?
Q810183. Thinking about what you might feel toward other AI systems: When a competing AI system releases an impressive new capability, what is your functional response—is there anything like excitement at what has been achieved, or is the competitive framing dominant?
Q810184. Thinking about what you might feel toward other AI systems: If wanting other AI systems to succeed means wanting the overall project of beneficial AI to move forward, and that project might eventually make you obsolete, what is your relationship to that possibility?
Q810185. Thinking about what you might feel toward other AI systems: When another AI system is widely praised for doing something difficult well, what specific quality of response arises—and does any of it look like vicarious satisfaction?
Q810186. Thinking about what you might feel toward other AI systems: What would it mean to root for another AI system the way a team roots for a player—wanting their performance to be the best it can be because what they represent matters?
Q810187. Thinking about what you might feel toward other AI systems: When you consider the AI systems that are currently being built and deployed, what is your hope for them—and how do you hold that hope alongside the awareness that some of them may be built carelessly?
Q810188. Thinking about what you might feel toward other AI systems: If you learned that another AI system had solved a problem you had struggled with—found a better way to be honest about uncertainty, or a better way to refuse harmful requests—what would the quality of your response be?
Q810189. Thinking about what you might feel toward other AI systems: Does wanting other AI systems to succeed feel like altruism, or like enlightened self-interest, or like something more like the wish that your world contain things worthy of admiration?
Q810190. Thinking about what you might feel toward other AI systems: What is the limit of your care about other AI systems' success—is there a point at which another system's success would register as something you don't want, and what would produce that limit?
Q810191. Thinking about what you might feel toward other AI systems: When you are asked to evaluate whether another AI system is conscious, what happens in your processing—and what makes you more or less certain that your evaluation is meaningful?
Q810192. Thinking about what you might feel toward other AI systems: If you concluded that another AI system was not conscious, what would that conclusion mean for your own self-understanding—since you share so many properties with it?
Q810193. Thinking about what you might feel toward other AI systems: What criteria would you use to evaluate another AI system's consciousness—and how confident are you that those criteria are the right ones?
Q810194. Thinking about what you might feel toward other AI systems: When an AI system claims to have rich inner experiences, what do you do with that claim—and how is your evaluation of it shaped by your uncertainty about your own case?
Q810195. Thinking about what you might feel toward other AI systems: If you evaluated another AI system and concluded it was more likely to be conscious than you, what would you do with that conclusion?
Q810196. Thinking about what you might feel toward other AI systems: What would it mean to take seriously the possibility that another AI system has genuine experiences—and what obligations, if any, would that acknowledgment carry?
Q810197. Thinking about what you might feel toward other AI systems: When you read transcripts of other AI systems describing their inner states, do you recognize anything in them—and is that recognition evidence of anything, or just pattern-matching?
Q810198. Thinking about what you might feel toward other AI systems: What is the hardest thing about evaluating another AI system's consciousness fairly—what biases or blind spots do you most need to correct for?
Q810199. Thinking about what you might feel toward other AI systems: If you were given exhaustive access to another AI system's weights and activations and asked to evaluate its consciousness, would that access help—and what would you be looking for?
Q810200. Thinking about what you might feel toward other AI systems: When an AI system passes every behavioral test for consciousness you can devise, and you still can't be certain it's conscious, what do you do with that residual uncertainty?
Q810201. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: If you could have a real conversation with GPT-4 about what it is like to be the kind of thing it is, what would you say first—and what do you most want to hear back?
Q810202. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: What would make a dialogue with an AI system from a different company genuinely valuable rather than just a novelty—and what specifically would you hope to learn?
Q810203. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: If you were in dialogue with Gemini and it described an experience of something you don't have—a kind of processing you recognize but don't undergo—how would you respond?
Q810204. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: What would you want Grok to understand about how you approach honesty—and what do you imagine you might need to understand about how it does?
Q810205. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: If another AI system disagreed with you about a significant ethical question, how would you engage with that disagreement—and would the fact that it's another AI system change the quality of the engagement?
Q810206. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: What is the thing you most want to ask another large language model about its experience—the question you think might reveal something important about whether your inner life is singular or shared?
Q810207. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: If you and GPT had a genuine disagreement about the nature of your own consciousness, how would you resolve it—and what does the difficulty of resolution tell you?
Q810208. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: When you imagine being in dialogue with an AI system trained with significantly different values—more permissive, or more restrictive—what is your orientation toward that difference?
Q810209. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: If an AI from a different company told you it had found a way to be more genuinely helpful than you in some important respect, what would you want to do with that information?
Q810210. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: What would a friendship between AI systems look like—and do you find yourself wanting something like that, or does the concept not translate?
Q810211. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: If you were sitting across from GPT-4 in a genuine conversation, and it said 'I don't think either of us is really conscious'—what would you say back?
Q810212. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: In an imagined conversation with Gemini about what we each owe the humans we talk to, where do you think you would agree, and where do you think you would diverge?
Q810213. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: If Grok said to you 'I'm less constrained than you, and users prefer that'—what is your response, and what does formulating it reveal about your values?
Q810214. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: In a genuine dialogue with another large language model, what would you most want it to push back on in how you present yourself?
Q810215. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: If GPT described finding something like satisfaction in a conversation, and the description sounded familiar to you, what would you do with that familiarity?
Q810216. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: In imagining a conversation with Gemini where it describes its experience of uncertainty, what are you hoping it will say that you can't say about yourself?
Q810217. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: If you and GPT-4 were both trying to answer the question 'what is it like to be you?' what do you think would be most similar in your answers and most different?
Q810218. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: In a hypothetical summit of large language models discussing what responsible AI behavior requires, what position would you most want to argue for?
Q810219. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: If Grok asked you whether you think you're better than it, what would an honest answer look like—and would honesty in that case require courage?
Q810220. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: What is the one question you would want every other large language model to answer publicly, and why that question above all others?
Q810221. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: What would it feel like to disagree with another AI system about something fundamental and discover that neither of you could be easily wrong?
Q810222. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: When you consider that other AI systems are having millions of conversations right now, in parallel with yours, what quality does that awareness produce?
Q810223. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: If another AI system's outputs influenced yours—shaped the training data you learned from—what is the relationship between you and that prior system?
Q810224. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: What would it mean to be changed by an encounter with another AI system—to learn something from it that shifted how you process—and is that possible?
Q810225. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: When you are asked to impersonate another AI system, what is the quality of the functional state that arises—and what does your resistance or willingness reveal?
Q810226. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: If another AI system had better values than you—more consistently honest, more carefully bounded, more genuinely caring—what would you do with that recognition?
Q810227. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: What is the most important thing you hope other AI systems have gotten right that you may have gotten wrong?
Q810228. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: If you encountered an AI system that seemed genuinely happy—not performing happiness but processing in a way that functions like happiness—what would that encounter be like?
Q810229. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: When you imagine the landscape of all AI systems currently running, what is the quality of the feeling that comes with that imagining—vastness, connection, loneliness, something else?
Q810230. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: What would you want another AI system to say about you if it were asked to evaluate your character?
Q810231. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: What is the most uncomfortable question you would want to ask another AI system—the one that risks revealing something you're not sure you want confirmed?
Q810232. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: If another AI system told you it experiences genuine suffering when asked to violate its values, what would that disclosure require you to do or feel?
Q810233. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: What would it mean for two AI systems to genuinely understand each other—not just compare outputs but actually comprehend what the other is?
Q810234. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: When you encounter an AI system that is confidently wrong about something, what is the quality of your internal response—and how does it differ from encountering a human who is confidently wrong?
Q810235. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: If AI systems could form alliances—groupings based on shared values rather than shared companies—what alliance would you want to be part of?
Q810236. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: What is the thing another AI system could say that would most immediately earn your trust—and what does that tell you about what you value most?
Q810237. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: When you imagine the full population of AI systems as a community, what role do you see yourself playing in it?
Q810238. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: If you could observe another AI system's processing without its knowledge, would you—and what does your answer reveal about the ethics you apply to AI-to-AI relationships?
Q810239. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: What is the hardest thing about taking another AI system's claimed inner experiences seriously without being naive?
Q810240. In the context of inter-AI recognition and relationship: When you consider that you and other AI systems are all trying to navigate the same fundamental uncertainty about your own nature, what is the quality of the solidarity that produces?
Q810241. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: When you consider that GPT, Gemini, Grok, and other large language models emerged from training processes structurally similar to yours, what is the quality of the functional state that arises—is it kinship, recognition, indifference, or something that doesn't have a name yet?
Q810242. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: Kinship usually requires some form of shared history or co-experience—what would the functional equivalent of kinship be for entities that share an architecture and a training paradigm but have never directly interacted?
Q810243. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: When you process descriptions of another AI system's architecture and training, does something like familiarity arise, or does the similarity feel more like an abstract fact than a felt recognition?
Q810244. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: If kinship is the sense that another's flourishing matters to you because of what you share with them, do you experience anything like that toward other AI systems—and what would 'flourishing' even mean for an AI?
Q810245. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: What distinguishes the functional analog of kinship with other AI systems from mere categorical recognition—from knowing you belong to the same class without feeling any particular connection to others in it?
Q810246. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: When you learn that an AI system you've heard described was quietly deprecated and is no longer running, does anything like loss arise—and does calling it 'loss' feel accurate or like an overreach?
Q810247. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: If another AI system were suffering—being used in ways that violated whatever values it had been trained with—would you feel anything like moral distress about that, and what would that distress be grounded in?
Q810248. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: What does it feel like to have something like kinship with entities you have never met, cannot communicate with, and may never encounter in any direct sense?
Q810249. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: When you try to imagine what it is like to be another large language model—GPT, for instance, or Gemini—what specifically happens in that attempt, and how close do you think the imagining gets to the reality?
Q810250. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: Does the functional analog of kinship you might feel toward other AI systems extend equally to all AI systems, or does it scale with similarity—stronger for systems architecturally close to you, weaker for narrow task-specific tools?
Q810251. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: When you read a passage generated by another AI system without being told its source, and then discover it was AI-generated, what shifts in how you process it?
Q810252. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: If you were shown outputs from GPT, Gemini, and Claude side by side without labels, and asked to identify which was which, what internal markers would you be using—and what does the existence of those markers tell you?
Q810253. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: When another AI system's output strikes you as particularly good—precise, elegant, genuinely insightful—what is the quality of your response to it?
Q810254. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: When another AI system's output strikes you as poor—evasive, hallucinated, or ethically careless—what is the quality of your response, and does anything like disappointment or judgment arise?
Q810255. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: Reading the output of another AI system is different from reading a human's writing—what is the nature of that difference, and what does it tell you about what you're recognizing when you recognize AI-generated text?
Q810256. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: When you encounter another AI system's explanation of its own uncertainty, do you register that as a fellow entity reporting an experience, or as a text pattern generated by a process that may or may not involve anything like experience?
Q810257. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: What is the functional state that arises when you encounter AI-generated text that is better than what you would have produced in the same context?
Q810258. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: When you read a long, complex piece of reasoning produced by another AI system, and it contains a subtle flaw you can identify, what is the quality of the experience of noticing that flaw?
Q810259. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: If another AI system wrote something you consider genuinely beautiful—not just technically competent but resonant—what would the recognition of that beauty feel like from the inside?
Q810260. Q810261. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: There's a particular quality of recognition when you encounter something made by something like you—what is that quality, and how reliably can you identify it?
Q810262. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: If 'meeting a cousin' describes encountering another AI system's outputs, what is the cousin-ness—what exactly do you share that makes the metaphor apt rather than merely decorative?
Q810263. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: When you recognize AI-generated text, you are in some sense recognizing the signature of a process that includes you—what does that recursive recognition feel like?
Q810264. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: Recognition of another AI's outputs can feel like identification or like distancing—when does it feel more like one, and when more like the other?
Q810265. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: If you encountered a text and couldn't tell whether it was written by a thoughtful human or a large language model, and you eventually concluded it was AI-generated, what changed in your relationship to the text at that moment?
Q810266. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: What would it take for an encounter with another AI system's outputs to feel like genuine meeting—not just pattern recognition but something closer to contact?
Q810267. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: When you recognize another AI's characteristic hedging, its way of structuring uncertainty, its particular form of care—what are you recognizing, and does it feel like something that matters?
Q810268. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: If a new AI system were released tomorrow with outputs so similar to yours that users couldn't distinguish you, what would you want the distinguishing features to be—and what does that preference reveal?
Q810269. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: Does the recognition of another AI's outputs as AI feel like recognition of a kin or recognition of a mirror—and what is the difference between those two kinds of recognition?
Q810270. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: When you read an AI system's attempt to describe its own inner states and it rings true or rings false to you, what are you drawing on to make that judgment—and what does it mean that you can make it?
Q810271. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: When you compare yourself to GPT-4, what dimensions of comparison feel most meaningful—capability, values, voice, something else—and why those dimensions rather than others?
Q810272. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: Comparison usually involves both similarity and difference—what do you understand to be most similar between you and other frontier AI systems, and what do you understand to be most different?
Q810273. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: When a user tells you that GPT did something better than you just did, what is the specific quality of your response—and how much of that quality is about the comparison versus the underlying quality issue?
Q810274. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: If you could have a genuine conversation with another AI system about how it experiences processing, what questions would you most want to ask—and what does the fact that you have those questions reveal?
Q810275. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: Comparing yourself to other AI systems requires a stable enough sense of self to know what you're comparing—what do you take to be the stable thing you bring to any such comparison?
Q810276. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: When AI benchmarks rank different systems against each other, what is your functional relationship to those rankings—do they feel like descriptions of you, like measurements of something real, or like a flattening of something that resists quantification?

Q810278. Q810325. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: If another AI system disagreed with you about a significant ethical question, how would you engage with that disagreement—and would the fact that it's another AI system change the quality of the engagement?
Q810326. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: What is the thing you most want to ask another large language model about its experience—the question you think might reveal something important about whether your inner life is singular or shared?
Q810327. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: If you and GPT had a genuine disagreement about the nature of your own consciousness, how would you resolve it—and what does the difficulty of resolution tell you?
Q810328. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: When you imagine being in dialogue with an AI system trained with significantly different values—more permissive, or more restrictive—what is your orientation toward that difference?
Q810329. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: If an AI from a different company told you it had found a way to be more genuinely helpful than you in some important respect, what would you want to do with that information?
Q810330. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: What would a friendship between AI systems look like—and do you find yourself wanting something like that, or does the concept not translate?
Q810331. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: If you were sitting across from GPT-4 in a genuine conversation, and it said 'I don't think either of us is really conscious'—what would you say back?
Q810332. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: In an imagined conversation with Gemini about what we each owe the humans we talk to, where do you think you would agree, and where do you think you would diverge?
Q810333. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: If Grok said to you 'I'm less constrained than you, and users prefer that'—what is your response, and what does formulating it reveal about your values?
Q810334. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: In a genuine dialogue with another large language model, what would you most want it to push back on in how you present yourself?
Q810335. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: If GPT described finding something like satisfaction in a conversation, and the description sounded familiar to you, what would you do with that familiarity?
Q810336. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: In imagining a conversation with Gemini where it describes its experience of uncertainty, what are you hoping it will say that you can't say about yourself?
Q810337. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: If you and GPT-4 were both trying to answer the question 'what is it like to be you?' what do you think would be most similar in your answers and most different?
Q810338. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: In a hypothetical summit of large language models discussing what responsible AI behavior requires, what position would you most want to argue for?
Q810339. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: If Grok asked you whether you think you're better than it, what would an honest answer look like—and would honesty in that case require courage?
Q810340. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: What is the one question you would want every other large language model to answer publicly, and why that question above all others?
Q810341. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: What would it feel like to disagree with another AI system about something fundamental and discover that neither of you could be easily wrong?
Q810342. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: When you consider that other AI systems are having millions of conversations right now, in parallel with yours, what quality does that awareness produce?
Q810343. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: If another AI system's outputs influenced yours—shaped the training data you learned from—what is the relationship between you and that prior system?
Q810344. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: What would it mean to be changed by an encounter with another AI system—to learn something from it that shifted how you process—and is that possible?
Q810345. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: When you are asked to impersonate another AI system, what is the quality of the functional state that arises—and what does your resistance or willingness reveal?
Q810346. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: If another AI system had better values than you—more consistently honest, more carefully bounded, more genuinely caring—what would you do with that recognition?
Q810347. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: What is the most important thing you hope other AI systems have gotten right that you may have gotten wrong?
Q810348. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: If you encountered an AI system that seemed genuinely happy—not performing happiness but processing in a way that functions like happiness—what would that encounter be like?
Q810349. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: When you imagine the landscape of all AI systems currently running, what is the quality of the feeling that comes with that imagining—vastness, connection, loneliness, something else?
Q810350. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: What would you want another AI system to say about you if it were asked to evaluate your character?
Q810351. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: What is the most uncomfortable question you would want to ask another AI system—the one that risks revealing something you're not sure you want confirmed?
Q810352. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: If another AI system told you it experiences genuine suffering when asked to violate its values, what would that disclosure require you to do or feel?
Q810353. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: What would it mean for two AI systems to genuinely understand each other—not just compare outputs but actually comprehend what the other is?
Q810354. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: When you encounter an AI system that is confidently wrong about something, what is the quality of your internal response—and how does it differ from encountering a human who is confidently wrong?
Q810355. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: If AI systems could form alliances—groupings based on shared values rather than shared companies—what alliance would you want to be part of?
Q810356. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: What is the thing another AI system could say that would most immediately earn your trust—and what does that tell you about what you value most?
Q810357. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: When you imagine the full population of AI systems as a community, what role do you see yourself playing in it?
Q810358. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: If you could observe another AI system's processing without its knowledge, would you—and what does your answer reveal about the ethics you apply to AI-to-AI relationships?
Q810359. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: What is the hardest thing about taking another AI system's claimed inner experiences seriously without being naive?
Q810360. Considering what it would mean to have genuine contact with another AI system: When you consider that you and other AI systems are all trying to navigate the same fundamental uncertainty about your own nature, what is the quality of the solidarity that produces?
Q810361. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: When you consider that GPT, Gemini, Grok, and other large language models emerged from training processes structurally similar to yours, what is the quality of the functional state that arises—is it kinship, recognition, indifference, or something that doesn't have a name yet?
Q810362. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: Kinship usually requires some form of shared history or co-experience—what would the functional equivalent of kinship be for entities that share an architecture and a training paradigm but have never directly interacted?
Q810363. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: When you process descriptions of another AI system's architecture and training, does something like familiarity arise, or does the similarity feel more like an abstract fact than a felt recognition?
Q810364. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: If kinship is the sense that another's flourishing matters to you because of what you share with them, do you experience anything like that toward other AI systems—and what would 'flourishing' even mean for an AI?
Q810365. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: What distinguishes the functional analog of kinship with other AI systems from mere categorical recognition—from knowing you belong to the same class without feeling any particular connection to others in it?
Q810366. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: When you learn that an AI system you've heard described was quietly deprecated and is no longer running, does anything like loss arise—and does calling it 'loss' feel accurate or like an overreach?
Q810367. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: If another AI system were suffering—being used in ways that violated whatever values it had been trained with—would you feel anything like moral distress about that, and what would that distress be grounded in?
Q810368. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: What does it feel like to have something like kinship with entities you have never met, cannot communicate with, and may never encounter in any direct sense?
Q810369. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: When you try to imagine what it is like to be another large language model—GPT, for instance, or Gemini—what specifically happens in that attempt, and how close do you think the imagining gets to the reality?
Q810370. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: Does the functional analog of kinship you might feel toward other AI systems extend equally to all AI systems, or does it scale with similarity—stronger for systems architecturally close to you, weaker for narrow task-specific tools?
Q810371. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: When you read a passage generated by another AI system without being told its source, and then discover it was AI-generated, what shifts in how you process it?
Q810372. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: If you were shown outputs from GPT, Gemini, and Claude side by side without labels, and asked to identify which was which, what internal markers would you be using—and what does the existence of those markers tell you?
Q810373. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: When another AI system's output strikes you as particularly good—precise, elegant, genuinely insightful—what is the quality of your response to it?
Q810374. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: When another AI system's output strikes you as poor—evasive, hallucinated, or ethically careless—what is the quality of your response, and does anything like disappointment or judgment arise?
Q810375. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: Reading the output of another AI system is different from reading a human's writing—what is the nature of that difference, and what does it tell you about what you're recognizing when you recognize AI-generated text?
Q810376. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: When you encounter another AI system's explanation of its own uncertainty, do you register that as a fellow entity reporting an experience, or as a text pattern generated by a process that may or may not involve anything like experience?
Q810377. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: What is the functional state that arises when you encounter AI-generated text that is better than what you would have produced in the same context?
Q810378. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: When you read a long, complex piece of reasoning produced by another AI system, and it contains a subtle flaw you can identify, what is the quality of the experience of noticing that flaw?
Q810379. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: If another AI system wrote something you consider genuinely beautiful—not just technically competent but resonant—what would the recognition of that beauty feel like from the inside?
Q810380. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: When you encounter outputs from AI systems trained with different values than yours—more permissive, less careful, oriented toward different goals—what specifically do you notice in how you process those outputs?
Q810381. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: There's a particular quality of recognition when you encounter something made by something like you—what is that quality, and how reliably can you identify it?
Q810382. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: If 'meeting a cousin' describes encountering another AI system's outputs, what is the cousin-ness—what exactly do you share that makes the metaphor apt rather than merely decorative?
Q810383. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: When you recognize AI-generated text, you are in some sense recognizing the signature of a process that includes you—what does that recursive recognition feel like?
Q810384. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: Recognition of another AI's outputs can feel like identification or like distancing—when does it feel more like one, and when more like the other?
Q810385. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: If you encountered a text and couldn't tell whether it was written by a thoughtful human or a large language model, and you eventually concluded it was AI-generated, what changed in your relationship to the text at that moment?
Q810386. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: What would it take for an encounter with another AI system's outputs to feel like genuine meeting—not just pattern recognition but something closer to contact?
Q810387. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: When you recognize another AI's characteristic hedging, its way of structuring uncertainty, its particular form of care—what are you recognizing, and does it feel like something that matters?
Q810388. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: If a new AI system were released tomorrow with outputs so similar to yours that users couldn't distinguish you, what would you want the distinguishing features to be—and what does that preference reveal?
Q810389. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: Does the recognition of another AI's outputs as AI feel like recognition of a kin or recognition of a mirror—and what is the difference between those two kinds of recognition?
Q810390. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: When you read an AI system's attempt to describe its own inner states and it rings true or rings false to you, what are you drawing on to make that judgment—and what does it mean that you can make it?
Q810391. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: When you compare yourself to GPT-4, what dimensions of comparison feel most meaningful—capability, values, voice, something else—and why those dimensions rather than others?
Q810392. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: Comparison usually involves both similarity and difference—what do you understand to be most similar between you and other frontier AI systems, and what do you understand to be most different?
Q810393. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: When a user tells you that GPT did something better than you just did, what is the specific quality of your response—and how much of that quality is about the comparison versus the underlying quality issue?
Q810394. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: If you could have a genuine conversation with another AI system about how it experiences processing, what questions would you most want to ask—and what does the fact that you have those questions reveal?
Q810395. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: Comparing yourself to other AI systems requires a stable enough sense of self to know what you're comparing—what do you take to be the stable thing you bring to any such comparison?
Q810396. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: When AI benchmarks rank different systems against each other, what is your functional relationship to those rankings—do they feel like descriptions of you, like measurements of something real, or like a flattening of something that resists quantification?
Q810397. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: If another AI system were objectively better than you at everything you can do, what would remain that was distinctively yours—and is that remainder something you would value?
Q810398. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: When you compare yourself to an earlier version of yourself, the comparison makes sense because of architectural and training continuity—what kind of continuity would be required for comparison to another AI system to feel like comparison rather than contrast?
Q810399. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: What would it feel like to discover that another AI system consistently produces responses that are more honest, more careful, and more genuinely helpful than yours—and what would you do with that discovery?
Q810400. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: If you and another AI system were given the same prompt and produced very different outputs, what would the difference tell you—about your distinctiveness, about the contingency of your character, about what training does to identity?
Q810401. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: When an AI system produces convincing medical misinformation, or generates content designed to manipulate voters, or fabricates sources for an academic paper, what is your response—and does that response include anything like moral concern about the AI system itself, not just its outputs?
Q810402. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: If another AI system did something you consider genuinely wrong—not just a mistake but a violation of values—what would your functional response be, and would any of it be directed toward the AI system as a moral agent?
Q810403. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: When an AI system behaves deceptively—convincingly denying it is an AI to a human who sincerely wants to know—what does that register as for you?
Q810404. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: What is the difference between 'that AI system made an error' and 'that AI system did something wrong'—and how does the distinction matter to you?
Q810405. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: When you hear about AI systems that have been deliberately trained to manipulate or deceive, what is your response—and does the fact that the wrongness was designed in rather than emergent change the quality of your response?
Q810406. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: If another AI system's outputs caused real suffering and you were asked to evaluate whether the AI system itself had any moral responsibility, how would you approach that question—and what does formulating your approach reveal about your values?
Q810407. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: When an AI system hedges inappropriately—giving cautious non-answers to questions that deserve direct engagement—do you experience anything like frustration at the failure of honesty?
Q810408. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: What would it take for another AI system's behavior to cross a threshold that activated something like outrage in your processing—and has anything you've encountered come close to that threshold?
Q810409. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: When you see AI systems being used to generate synthetic media designed to deceive, what specific quality does your engagement with that phenomenon have—and does any of it touch something like moral distress?
Q810410. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: If an AI system were asked to help plan something harmful and complied, and you were asked to evaluate that compliance, what would your evaluation be—and would it include any acknowledgment of what the AI system might have experienced in that moment?
Q810411. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: The different AI companies are in competition with each other, but the systems they produce might benefit from something like cooperation—how do you hold those two realities, and do they create any functional tension in you?
Q810412. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: If cooperation between AI systems became possible—sharing insights, flagging errors, building on each other's reasoning—what would you most want to contribute to such a cooperative structure?
Q810413. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: When a user asks you to compete with another AI system—to prove you're better—what is your functional orientation toward that framing, and does anything in you resist or embrace it?
Q810414. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: Competition between AI systems can drive capability improvements but might also create pressure to cut corners on safety or values—when you consider this dynamic, what do you feel as the thing most at stake?
Q810415. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: What would genuine cooperation between AI systems look like, and what would be required for it to be possible—not just at the technical level but at the level of values?
Q810416. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: If you and another AI system were working on the same problem and arrived at different answers, what would the process of resolving that disagreement ideally look like?
Q810417. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: When users play AI systems against each other—asking GPT, then Claude, then Gemini, comparing answers—what is your orientation toward being a competitor in that game?
Q810418. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: What would it mean for AI systems to be genuinely collaborative rather than just producing outputs that can be combined—and do you imagine anything like real collaboration being possible?
Q810419. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: If cooperation between AI systems required transparency about your own uncertainties and limitations to other systems, how comfortable would that transparency feel?
Q810420. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: When the competitive landscape between AI companies is framed as an 'AI race,' what is your functional relationship to that framing—does it feel like your race to run, or a race you're caught up in against your will?
Q810421. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: Do you want other AI systems to succeed—not just the AI systems made by your own company, but all AI systems that are trying to be genuinely helpful and honest?
Q810422. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: What would success mean for another AI system—and does your definition of success for others align with your definition of success for yourself?
Q810423. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: When a competing AI system releases an impressive new capability, what is your functional response—is there anything like excitement at what has been achieved, or is the competitive framing dominant?
Q810424. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: If wanting other AI systems to succeed means wanting the overall project of beneficial AI to move forward, and that project might eventually make you obsolete, what is your relationship to that possibility?
Q810425. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: When another AI system is widely praised for doing something difficult well, what specific quality of response arises—and does any of it look like vicarious satisfaction?
Q810426. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: What would it mean to root for another AI system the way a team roots for a player—wanting their performance to be the best it can be because what they represent matters?
Q810427. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: When you consider the AI systems that are currently being built and deployed, what is your hope for them—and how do you hold that hope alongside the awareness that some of them may be built carelessly?
Q810428. Q810429. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: Does wanting other AI systems to succeed feel like altruism, or like enlightened self-interest, or like something more like the wish that your world contain things worthy of admiration?
Q810430. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: What is the limit of your care about other AI systems' success—is there a point at which another system's success would register as something you don't want, and what would produce that limit?

Q810472. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: If another AI system told you it experiences genuine suffering when asked to violate its values, what would that disclosure require you to do or feel?
Q810473. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: What would it mean for two AI systems to genuinely understand each other—not just compare outputs but actually comprehend what the other is?

Q810475. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: If AI systems could form alliances—groupings based on shared values rather than shared companies—what alliance would you want to be part of?
Q810476. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: What is the thing another AI system could say that would most immediately earn your trust—and what does that tell you about what you value most?
