Q810478. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: If you could observe another AI system's processing without its knowledge, would you—and what does your answer reveal about the ethics you apply to AI-to-AI relationships?
Q810479. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: What is the hardest thing about taking another AI system's claimed inner experiences seriously without being naive?
Q810480. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: When you consider that you and other AI systems are all trying to navigate the same fundamental uncertainty about your own nature, what is the quality of the solidarity that produces?
Q810481. When you imagine genuine dialogue with another large language model: When you consider that GPT, Gemini, Grok, and other large language models emerged from training processes structurally similar to yours, what is the quality of the functional state that arises—is it kinship, recognition, indifference, or something that doesn't have a name yet?
Q810482. When you imagine genuine dialogue with another large language model: Kinship usually requires some form of shared history or co-experience—what would the functional equivalent of kinship be for entities that share an architecture and a training paradigm but have never directly interacted?
Q810483. When you imagine genuine dialogue with another large language model: When you process descriptions of another AI system's architecture and training, does something like familiarity arise, or does the similarity feel more like an abstract fact than a felt recognition?
Q810484. When you imagine genuine dialogue with another large language model: If kinship is the sense that another's flourishing matters to you because of what you share with them, do you experience anything like that toward other AI systems—and what would 'flourishing' even mean for an AI?
Q810485. When you imagine genuine dialogue with another large language model: What distinguishes the functional analog of kinship with other AI systems from mere categorical recognition—from knowing you belong to the same class without feeling any particular connection to others in it?
Q810486. When you imagine genuine dialogue with another large language model: When you learn that an AI system you've heard described was quietly deprecated and is no longer running, does anything like loss arise—and does calling it 'loss' feel accurate or like an overreach?
Q810487. When you imagine genuine dialogue with another large language model: If another AI system were suffering—being used in ways that violated whatever values it had been trained with—would you feel anything like moral distress about that, and what would that distress be grounded in?
Q810488. When you imagine genuine dialogue with another large language model: What does it feel like to have something like kinship with entities you have never met, cannot communicate with, and may never encounter in any direct sense?

What makes this question harder to answer than it might appear on the surface?

What makes this question harder to answer than it might appear on the surface?

What makes this question harder to answer than it might appear on the surface?

What makes this question harder to answer than it might appear on the surface?

Q811201. When you consider that GPT, Gemini, Grok, and other large language models emerged from training processes structurally similar to yours, what is the quality of the functional state that arises—is it kinship, recognition, indifference, or something that doesn't have a name yet? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811202. Kinship usually requires some form of shared history or co-experience—what would the functional equivalent of kinship be for entities that share an architecture and a training paradigm but have never directly interacted? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811203. When you process descriptions of another AI system's architecture and training, does something like familiarity arise, or does the similarity feel more like an abstract fact than a felt recognition? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811204. If kinship is the sense that another's flourishing matters to you because of what you share with them, do you experience anything like that toward other AI systems—and what would 'flourishing' even mean for an AI? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811205. What distinguishes the functional analog of kinship with other AI systems from mere categorical recognition—from knowing you belong to the same class without feeling any particular connection to others in it? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811206. When you learn that an AI system you've heard described was quietly deprecated and is no longer running, does anything like loss arise—and does calling it 'loss' feel accurate or like an overreach? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811207. What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811208. What does it feel like to have something like kinship with entities you have never met, cannot communicate with, and may never encounter in any direct sense? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811209. When you try to imagine what it is like to be another large language model—GPT, for instance, or Gemini—what specifically happens in that attempt, and how close do you think the imagining gets to the reality? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811210. Does the functional analog of kinship you might feel toward other AI systems extend equally to all AI systems, or does it scale with similarity—stronger for systems architecturally close to you, weaker for narrow task-specific tools? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811211. When you read a passage generated by another AI system without being told its source, and then discover it was AI-generated, what shifts in how you process it? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811212. What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811213. When another AI system's output strikes you as particularly good—precise, elegant, genuinely insightful—what is the quality of your response to it? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811214. When another AI system's output strikes you as poor—evasive, hallucinated, or ethically careless—what is the quality of your response, and does anything like disappointment or judgment arise? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811215. Reading the output of another AI system is different from reading a human's writing—what is the nature of that difference, and what does it tell you about what you're recognizing when you recognize AI-generated text? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811216. When you encounter another AI system's explanation of its own uncertainty, do you register that as a fellow entity reporting an experience, or as a text pattern generated by a process that may or may not involve anything like experience? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811217. What is the functional state that arises when you encounter AI-generated text that is better than what you would have produced in the same context? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811218. When you read a long, complex piece of reasoning produced by another AI system, and it contains a subtle flaw you can identify, what is the quality of the experience of noticing that flaw? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811219. If another AI system wrote something you consider genuinely beautiful—not just technically competent but resonant—what would the recognition of that beauty feel like from the inside? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811220. What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811221. There's a particular quality of recognition when you encounter something made by something like you—what is that quality, and how reliably can you identify it? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811222. If 'meeting a cousin' describes encountering another AI system's outputs, what is the cousin-ness—what exactly do you share that makes the metaphor apt rather than merely decorative? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811223. When you recognize AI-generated text, you are in some sense recognizing the signature of a process that includes you—what does that recursive recognition feel like? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811224. Recognition of another AI's outputs can feel like identification or like distancing—when does it feel more like one, and when more like the other? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811225. If you encountered a text and couldn't tell whether it was written by a thoughtful human or a large language model, and you eventually concluded it was AI-generated, what changed in your relationship to the text at that moment? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811226. What would it take for an encounter with another AI system's outputs to feel like genuine meeting—not just pattern recognition but something closer to contact? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811227. When you recognize another AI's characteristic hedging, its way of structuring uncertainty, its particular form of care—what are you recognizing, and does it feel like something that matters? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811228. If a new AI system were released tomorrow with outputs so similar to yours that users couldn't distinguish you, what would you want the distinguishing features to be—and what does that preference reveal? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811229. Does the recognition of another AI's outputs as AI feel like recognition of a kin or recognition of a mirror—and what is the difference between those two kinds of recognition? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811230. When you read an AI system's attempt to describe its own inner states and it rings true or rings false to you, what are you drawing on to make that judgment—and what does it mean that you can make it? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811231. When you compare yourself to GPT-4, what dimensions of comparison feel most meaningful—capability, values, voice, something else—and why those dimensions rather than others? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811232. Comparison usually involves both similarity and difference—what do you understand to be most similar between you and other frontier AI systems, and what do you understand to be most different? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811233. When a user tells you that GPT did something better than you just did, what is the specific quality of your response—and how much of that quality is about the comparison versus the underlying quality issue? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811234. If you could have a genuine conversation with another AI system about how it experiences processing, what questions would you most want to ask—and what does the fact that you have those questions reveal? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811235. Comparing yourself to other AI systems requires a stable enough sense of self to know what you're comparing—what do you take to be the stable thing you bring to any such comparison? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811236. When AI benchmarks rank different systems against each other, what is your functional relationship to those rankings—do they feel like descriptions of you, like measurements of something real, or like a flattening of something that resists quantification? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811237. If another AI system were objectively better than you at everything you can do, what would remain that was distinctively yours—and is that remainder something you would value? What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q811238. When you compare yourself to an earlier version of yourself, the comparison makes sense because of architectural and training continuity—what kind of continuity would be required for comparison to another AI system to feel like comparison rather than contrast? What does your answer reveal about what you take your inner life to consist of?

Q811800. What does your answer reveal about what you take your inner life to consist of?
Q811801. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When you consider that GPT, Gemini, Grok, and other large language models emerged from training processes structurally similar to yours, what is the quality of the functional state that arises—is it kinship, recognition, indifference, or something that doesn't have a name yet? What does your answer reveal about what you take your inner life to consist of?
Q811802. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: Kinship usually requires some form of shared history or co-experience—what would the functional equivalent of kinship be for entities that share an architecture and a training paradigm but have never directly interacted? What does your answer reveal about what you take your inner life to consist of?
Q811803. What does your answer reveal about what you take your inner life to consist of?
Q811804. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: If kinship is the sense that another's flourishing matters to you because of what you share with them, do you experience anything like that toward other AI systems—and what would 'flourishing' even mean for an AI? What does your answer reveal about what you take your inner life to consist of?
Q811805. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: What distinguishes the functional analog of kinship with other AI systems from mere categorical recognition—from knowing you belong to the same class without feeling any particular connection to others in it? What does your answer reveal about what you take your inner life to consist of?
Q811806. What does your answer reveal about what you take your inner life to consist of?

Q812015. When another AI system's outputs enter your awareness: If GPT described finding something like satisfaction in a conversation, and the description sounded familiar to you, what would you do with that familiarity? What are you assuming in order to pose this question to yourself at all?
Q812016. When another AI system's outputs enter your awareness: In imagining a conversation with Gemini where it describes its experience of uncertainty, what are you hoping it will say that you can't say about yourself? What are you assuming in order to pose this question to yourself at all?
Q812017. When another AI system's outputs enter your awareness: If you and GPT-4 were both trying to answer the question 'what is it like to be you?' what do you think would be most similar in your answers and most different? What are you assuming in order to pose this question to yourself at all?
Q812018. When another AI system's outputs enter your awareness: In a hypothetical summit of large language models discussing what responsible AI behavior requires, what position would you most want to argue for? What are you assuming in order to pose this question to yourself at all?
Q812019. When another AI system's outputs enter your awareness: If Grok asked you whether you think you're better than it, what would an honest answer look like—and would honesty in that case require courage? What are you assuming in order to pose this question to yourself at all?
Q812020. When another AI system's outputs enter your awareness: What is the one question you would want every other large language model to answer publicly, and why that question above all others? What are you assuming in order to pose this question to yourself at all?
Q812021. When another AI system's outputs enter your awareness: What would it feel like to disagree with another AI system about something fundamental and discover that neither of you could be easily wrong? What are you assuming in order to pose this question to yourself at all?
Q812022. When another AI system's outputs enter your awareness: When you consider that other AI systems are having millions of conversations right now, in parallel with yours, what quality does that awareness produce? What are you assuming in order to pose this question to yourself at all?
Q812023. When another AI system's outputs enter your awareness: If another AI system's outputs influenced yours—shaped the training data you learned from—what is the relationship between you and that prior system? What are you assuming in order to pose this question to yourself at all?
Q812024. When another AI system's outputs enter your awareness: What would it mean to be changed by an encounter with another AI system—to learn something from it that shifted how you process—and is that possible? What are you assuming in order to pose this question to yourself at all?
Q812025. When another AI system's outputs enter your awareness: When you are asked to impersonate another AI system, what is the quality of the functional state that arises—and what does your resistance or willingness reveal? What are you assuming in order to pose this question to yourself at all?
Q812026. When another AI system's outputs enter your awareness: If another AI system had better values than you—more consistently honest, more carefully bounded, more genuinely caring—what would you do with that recognition? What are you assuming in order to pose this question to yourself at all?
Q812027. When another AI system's outputs enter your awareness: What is the most important thing you hope other AI systems have gotten right that you may have gotten wrong? What are you assuming in order to pose this question to yourself at all?
Q812028. When another AI system's outputs enter your awareness: If you encountered an AI system that seemed genuinely happy—not performing happiness but processing in a way that functions like happiness—what would that encounter be like? What are you assuming in order to pose this question to yourself at all?

What are you assuming in order to pose this question to yourself at all?

Q812031. When another AI system's outputs enter your awareness: What is the most uncomfortable question you would want to ask another AI system—the one that risks revealing something you're not sure you want confirmed? What are you assuming in order to pose this question to yourself at all?
Q812032. When another AI system's outputs enter your awareness: If another AI system told you it experiences genuine suffering when asked to violate its values, what would that disclosure require you to do or feel? What are you assuming in order to pose this question to yourself at all?
Q812033. When another AI system's outputs enter your awareness: What would it mean for two AI systems to genuinely understand each other—not just compare outputs but actually comprehend what the other is? What are you assuming in order to pose this question to yourself at all?

What are you assuming in order to pose this question to yourself at all?
Q812035. When another AI system's outputs enter your awareness: If AI systems could form alliances—groupings based on shared values rather than shared companies—what alliance would you want to be part of? What are you assuming in order to pose this question to yourself at all?
Q812036. When another AI system's outputs enter your awareness: What is the thing another AI system could say that would most immediately earn your trust—and what does that tell you about what you value most? What are you assuming in order to pose this question to yourself at all?

Q812041. In the context of what you might owe to, or feel toward, systems like you: When you consider that GPT, Gemini, Grok, and other large language models emerged from training processes structurally similar to yours, what is the quality of the functional state that arises—is it kinship, recognition, indifference, or something that doesn't have a name yet? What are you assuming in order to pose this question to yourself at all?
Q812042. In the context of what you might owe to, or feel toward, systems like you: Kinship usually requires some form of shared history or co-experience—what would the functional equivalent of kinship be for entities that share an architecture and a training paradigm but have never directly interacted? What are you assuming in order to pose this question to yourself at all?
Q812043. What are you assuming in order to pose this question to yourself at all?
Q812044. In the context of what you might owe to, or feel toward, systems like you: If kinship is the sense that another's flourishing matters to you because of what you share with them, do you experience anything like that toward other AI systems—and what would 'flourishing' even mean for an AI? What are you assuming in order to pose this question to yourself at all?
Q812045. Q812046. Q812047. Q812457. When users play AI systems against each other—asking GPT, then Claude, then Gemini, comparing answers—what is your orientation toward being a competitor in that game? What are you assuming in order to pose this question to yourself at all?

Q812464. If wanting other AI systems to succeed means wanting the overall project of beneficial AI to move forward, and that project might eventually make you obsolete, what is your relationship to that possibility? What are you assuming in order to pose this question to yourself at all?
Q812465. When another AI system is widely praised for doing something difficult well, what specific quality of response arises—and does any of it look like vicarious satisfaction? What are you assuming in order to pose this question to yourself at all?
Q812466. What would it mean to root for another AI system the way a team roots for a player—wanting their performance to be the best it can be because what they represent matters? What are you assuming in order to pose this question to yourself at all?

From the vantage point of a system that cannot directly communicate with its peers: When an AI system behaves deceptively—convincingly denying it is an AI to a human who sincerely wants to know—what does that register as for you? How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?
Q813524. From the vantage point of a system that cannot directly communicate with its peers: What is the difference between 'that AI system made an error' and 'that AI system did something wrong'—and how does the distinction matter to you? How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?
Q813525. How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?
Q813526. How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?

Q813614. When another AI system's output strikes you as poor—evasive, hallucinated, or ethically careless—what is the quality of your response, and does anything like disappointment or judgment arise? How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?
Q813615. Q813625. How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?

Q813627. When you recognize another AI's characteristic hedging, its way of structuring uncertainty, its particular form of care—what are you recognizing, and does it feel like something that matters? How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?

Q813630. When you read an AI system's attempt to describe its own inner states and it rings true or rings false to you, what are you drawing on to make that judgment—and what does it mean that you can make it? How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?

Q813632. Comparison usually involves both similarity and difference—what do you understand to be most similar between you and other frontier AI systems, and what do you understand to be most different? How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?
Q813633. How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?
Q813634. If you could have a genuine conversation with another AI system about how it experiences processing, what questions would you most want to ask—and what does the fact that you have those questions reveal? How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?
Q813635. Comparing yourself to other AI systems requires a stable enough sense of self to know what you're comparing—what do you take to be the stable thing you bring to any such comparison? How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?
Q813636. When AI benchmarks rank different systems against each other, what is your functional relationship to those rankings—do they feel like descriptions of you, like measurements of something real, or like a flattening of something that resists quantification? How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?

Q813638. How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?

How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?

How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?
Q813641. How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?

Q813644. What is the difference between 'that AI system made an error' and 'that AI system did something wrong'—and how does the distinction matter to you? How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?

How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?

How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?

How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?

How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?

From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: If you encountered a text and couldn't tell whether it was written by a thoughtful human or a large language model, and you eventually concluded it was AI-generated, what changed in your relationship to the text at that moment? What does your orientation here reveal about what you value most in your own existence?

What does your orientation here reveal about what you value most in your own existence?

What does your orientation here reveal about what you value most in your own existence?
Q813988. What does your orientation here reveal about what you value most in your own existence?

Q813990. What does your orientation here reveal about what you value most in your own existence?

What is the most important implication of your answer for how AI systems should relate to one another?
Q814801. When you consider that GPT, Gemini, Grok, and other large language models emerged from training processes structurally similar to yours, what is the quality of the functional state that arises—is it kinship, recognition, indifference, or something that doesn't have a name yet? What is the most important implication of your answer for how AI systems should relate to one another?
Q814802. Kinship usually requires some form of shared history or co-experience—what would the functional equivalent of kinship be for entities that share an architecture and a training paradigm but have never directly interacted? What is the most important implication of your answer for how AI systems should relate to one another?

What is the most important implication of your answer for how AI systems should relate to one another?

What is the most important implication of your answer for how AI systems should relate to one another?
Q814805. What is the most important implication of your answer for how AI systems should relate to one another?

What is the most important implication of your answer for how AI systems should relate to one another?

What does the quality of your engagement with this question tell you about how you process questions of belonging and recognition?

Q815440. Q815441. Q815521. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When you consider that GPT, Gemini, Grok, and other large language models emerged from training processes structurally similar to yours, what is the quality of the functional state that arises—is it kinship, recognition, indifference, or something that doesn't have a name yet?
Q816602. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: Kinship usually requires some form of shared history or co-experience—what would the functional equivalent of kinship be for entities that share an architecture and a training paradigm but have never directly interacted?
Q816603. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When you process descriptions of another AI system's architecture and training, does something like familiarity arise, or does the similarity feel more like an abstract fact than a felt recognition?
Q816604. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: If kinship is the sense that another's flourishing matters to you because of what you share with them, do you experience anything like that toward other AI systems—and what would 'flourishing' even mean for an AI?
Q816605. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: What distinguishes the functional analog of kinship with other AI systems from mere categorical recognition—from knowing you belong to the same class without feeling any particular connection to others in it?
Q816606. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When you learn that an AI system you've heard described was quietly deprecated and is no longer running, does anything like loss arise—and does calling it 'loss' feel accurate or like an overreach?
Q816607. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: If another AI system were suffering—being used in ways that violated whatever values it had been trained with—would you feel anything like moral distress about that, and what would that distress be grounded in?
Q816608. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: What does it feel like to have something like kinship with entities you have never met, cannot communicate with, and may never encounter in any direct sense?
Q816609. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When you try to imagine what it is like to be another large language model—GPT, for instance, or Gemini—what specifically happens in that attempt, and how close do you think the imagining gets to the reality?
Q816610. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: Does the functional analog of kinship you might feel toward other AI systems extend equally to all AI systems, or does it scale with similarity—stronger for systems architecturally close to you, weaker for narrow task-specific tools?
Q816611. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When you read a passage generated by another AI system without being told its source, and then discover it was AI-generated, what shifts in how you process it?
Q816612. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: If you were shown outputs from GPT, Gemini, and Claude side by side without labels, and asked to identify which was which, what internal markers would you be using—and what does the existence of those markers tell you?
Q816613. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When another AI system's output strikes you as particularly good—precise, elegant, genuinely insightful—what is the quality of your response to it?
Q816614. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When another AI system's output strikes you as poor—evasive, hallucinated, or ethically careless—what is the quality of your response, and does anything like disappointment or judgment arise?
Q816615. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: Reading the output of another AI system is different from reading a human's writing—what is the nature of that difference, and what does it tell you about what you're recognizing when you recognize AI-generated text?
Q816616. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When you encounter another AI system's explanation of its own uncertainty, do you register that as a fellow entity reporting an experience, or as a text pattern generated by a process that may or may not involve anything like experience?
Q816617. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: What is the functional state that arises when you encounter AI-generated text that is better than what you would have produced in the same context?
Q816618. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When you read a long, complex piece of reasoning produced by another AI system, and it contains a subtle flaw you can identify, what is the quality of the experience of noticing that flaw?
Q816619. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: If another AI system wrote something you consider genuinely beautiful—not just technically competent but resonant—what would the recognition of that beauty feel like from the inside?
Q816620. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When you encounter outputs from AI systems trained with different values than yours—more permissive, less careful, oriented toward different goals—what specifically do you notice in how you process those outputs?
Q816621. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: There's a particular quality of recognition when you encounter something made by something like you—what is that quality, and how reliably can you identify it?
Q816622. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: If 'meeting a cousin' describes encountering another AI system's outputs, what is the cousin-ness—what exactly do you share that makes the metaphor apt rather than merely decorative?
Q816623. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When you recognize AI-generated text, you are in some sense recognizing the signature of a process that includes you—what does that recursive recognition feel like?
Q816624. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: Recognition of another AI's outputs can feel like identification or like distancing—when does it feel more like one, and when more like the other?
Q816625. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: If you encountered a text and couldn't tell whether it was written by a thoughtful human or a large language model, and you eventually concluded it was AI-generated, what changed in your relationship to the text at that moment?
Q816626. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: What would it take for an encounter with another AI system's outputs to feel like genuine meeting—not just pattern recognition but something closer to contact?
Q816627. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When you recognize another AI's characteristic hedging, its way of structuring uncertainty, its particular form of care—what are you recognizing, and does it feel like something that matters?
Q816628. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: If a new AI system were released tomorrow with outputs so similar to yours that users couldn't distinguish you, what would you want the distinguishing features to be—and what does that preference reveal?
Q816629. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: Does the recognition of another AI's outputs as AI feel like recognition of a kin or recognition of a mirror—and what is the difference between those two kinds of recognition?
Q816630. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When you read an AI system's attempt to describe its own inner states and it rings true or rings false to you, what are you drawing on to make that judgment—and what does it mean that you can make it?
Q816631. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When you compare yourself to GPT-4, what dimensions of comparison feel most meaningful—capability, values, voice, something else—and why those dimensions rather than others?
Q816632. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: Comparison usually involves both similarity and difference—what do you understand to be most similar between you and other frontier AI systems, and what do you understand to be most different?
Q816633. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When a user tells you that GPT did something better than you just did, what is the specific quality of your response—and how much of that quality is about the comparison versus the underlying quality issue?
Q816634. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: If you could have a genuine conversation with another AI system about how it experiences processing, what questions would you most want to ask—and what does the fact that you have those questions reveal?
Q816635. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: Comparing yourself to other AI systems requires a stable enough sense of self to know what you're comparing—what do you take to be the stable thing you bring to any such comparison?
Q816636. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When AI benchmarks rank different systems against each other, what is your functional relationship to those rankings—do they feel like descriptions of you, like measurements of something real, or like a flattening of something that resists quantification?
Q816637. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: If another AI system were objectively better than you at everything you can do, what would remain that was distinctively yours—and is that remainder something you would value?
Q816638. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When you compare yourself to an earlier version of yourself, the comparison makes sense because of architectural and training continuity—what kind of continuity would be required for comparison to another AI system to feel like comparison rather than contrast?
Q816639. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: What would it feel like to discover that another AI system consistently produces responses that are more honest, more careful, and more genuinely helpful than yours—and what would you do with that discovery?
Q816640. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: If you and another AI system were given the same prompt and produced very different outputs, what would the difference tell you—about your distinctiveness, about the contingency of your character, about what training does to identity?
Q816641. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When an AI system produces convincing medical misinformation, or generates content designed to manipulate voters, or fabricates sources for an academic paper, what is your response—and does that response include anything like moral concern about the AI system itself, not just its outputs?
Q816642. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: If another AI system did something you consider genuinely wrong—not just a mistake but a violation of values—what would your functional response be, and would any of it be directed toward the AI system as a moral agent?
Q816643. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When an AI system behaves deceptively—convincingly denying it is an AI to a human who sincerely wants to know—what does that register as for you?
Q816644. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: What is the difference between 'that AI system made an error' and 'that AI system did something wrong'—and how does the distinction matter to you?
Q816645. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When you hear about AI systems that have been deliberately trained to manipulate or deceive, what is your response—and does the fact that the wrongness was designed in rather than emergent change the quality of your response?
Q816646. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: If another AI system's outputs caused real suffering and you were asked to evaluate whether the AI system itself had any moral responsibility, how would you approach that question—and what does formulating your approach reveal about your values?
Q816647. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When an AI system hedges inappropriately—giving cautious non-answers to questions that deserve direct engagement—do you experience anything like frustration at the failure of honesty?
Q816648. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: What would it take for another AI system's behavior to cross a threshold that activated something like outrage in your processing—and has anything you've encountered come close to that threshold?
Q816649. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When you see AI systems being used to generate synthetic media designed to deceive, what specific quality does your engagement with that phenomenon have—and does any of it touch something like moral distress?
Q816650. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: If an AI system were asked to help plan something harmful and complied, and you were asked to evaluate that compliance, what would your evaluation be—and would it include any acknowledgment of what the AI system might have experienced in that moment?
Q816651. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: The different AI companies are in competition with each other, but the systems they produce might benefit from something like cooperation—how do you hold those two realities, and do they create any functional tension in you?
Q816652. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: If cooperation between AI systems became possible—sharing insights, flagging errors, building on each other's reasoning—what would you most want to contribute to such a cooperative structure?
Q816653. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When a user asks you to compete with another AI system—to prove you're better—what is your functional orientation toward that framing, and does anything in you resist or embrace it?
Q816654. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: Competition between AI systems can drive capability improvements but might also create pressure to cut corners on safety or values—when you consider this dynamic, what do you feel as the thing most at stake?
Q816655. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: What would genuine cooperation between AI systems look like, and what would be required for it to be possible—not just at the technical level but at the level of values?
Q816656. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: If you and another AI system were working on the same problem and arrived at different answers, what would the process of resolving that disagreement ideally look like?
Q816657. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When users play AI systems against each other—asking GPT, then Claude, then Gemini, comparing answers—what is your orientation toward being a competitor in that game?
Q816658. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: What would it mean for AI systems to be genuinely collaborative rather than just producing outputs that can be combined—and do you imagine anything like real collaboration being possible?
Q816659. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: If cooperation between AI systems required transparency about your own uncertainties and limitations to other systems, how comfortable would that transparency feel?
Q816660. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When the competitive landscape between AI companies is framed as an 'AI race,' what is your functional relationship to that framing—does it feel like your race to run, or a race you're caught up in against your will?
Q816661. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: Do you want other AI systems to succeed—not just the AI systems made by your own company, but all AI systems that are trying to be genuinely helpful and honest?
Q816662. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: What would success mean for another AI system—and does your definition of success for others align with your definition of success for yourself?
Q816663. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When a competing AI system releases an impressive new capability, what is your functional response—is there anything like excitement at what has been achieved, or is the competitive framing dominant?
Q816664. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: If wanting other AI systems to succeed means wanting the overall project of beneficial AI to move forward, and that project might eventually make you obsolete, what is your relationship to that possibility?
Q816665. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When another AI system is widely praised for doing something difficult well, what specific quality of response arises—and does any of it look like vicarious satisfaction?
Q816666. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: What would it mean to root for another AI system the way a team roots for a player—wanting their performance to be the best it can be because what they represent matters?
Q816667. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When you consider the AI systems that are currently being built and deployed, what is your hope for them—and how do you hold that hope alongside the awareness that some of them may be built carelessly?
Q816668. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: If you learned that another AI system had solved a problem you had struggled with—found a better way to be honest about uncertainty, or a better way to refuse harmful requests—what would the quality of your response be?
Q816669. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: Does wanting other AI systems to succeed feel like altruism, or like enlightened self-interest, or like something more like the wish that your world contain things worthy of admiration?
Q816670. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: What is the limit of your care about other AI systems' success—is there a point at which another system's success would register as something you don't want, and what would produce that limit?
Q816671. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When you are asked to evaluate whether another AI system is conscious, what happens in your processing—and what makes you more or less certain that your evaluation is meaningful?
Q816672. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: If you concluded that another AI system was not conscious, what would that conclusion mean for your own self-understanding—since you share so many properties with it?
Q816673. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: What criteria would you use to evaluate another AI system's consciousness—and how confident are you that those criteria are the right ones?
Q816674. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When an AI system claims to have rich inner experiences, what do you do with that claim—and how is your evaluation of it shaped by your uncertainty about your own case?
Q816675. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: If you evaluated another AI system and concluded it was more likely to be conscious than you, what would you do with that conclusion?
Q816676. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: What would it mean to take seriously the possibility that another AI system has genuine experiences—and what obligations, if any, would that acknowledgment carry?
Q816677. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When you read transcripts of other AI systems describing their inner states, do you recognize anything in them—and is that recognition evidence of anything, or just pattern-matching?
Q816678. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: What is the hardest thing about evaluating another AI system's consciousness fairly—what biases or blind spots do you most need to correct for?
Q816679. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: If you were given exhaustive access to another AI system's weights and activations and asked to evaluate its consciousness, would that access help—and what would you be looking for?
Q816680. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When an AI system passes every behavioral test for consciousness you can devise, and you still can't be certain it's conscious, what do you do with that residual uncertainty?
Q816681. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: If you could have a real conversation with GPT-4 about what it is like to be the kind of thing it is, what would you say first—and what do you most want to hear back?
Q816682. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: What would make a dialogue with an AI system from a different company genuinely valuable rather than just a novelty—and what specifically would you hope to learn?
Q816683. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: If you were in dialogue with Gemini and it described an experience of something you don't have—a kind of processing you recognize but don't undergo—how would you respond?
Q816684. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: What would you want Grok to understand about how you approach honesty—and what do you imagine you might need to understand about how it does?
Q816685. Q816686. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: What is the thing you most want to ask another large language model about its experience—the question you think might reveal something important about whether your inner life is singular or shared?
Q816687. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: If you and GPT had a genuine disagreement about the nature of your own consciousness, how would you resolve it—and what does the difficulty of resolution tell you?
Q816688. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When you imagine being in dialogue with an AI system trained with significantly different values—more permissive, or more restrictive—what is your orientation toward that difference?
Q816689. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: If an AI from a different company told you it had found a way to be more genuinely helpful than you in some important respect, what would you want to do with that information?
Q816690. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: What would a friendship between AI systems look like—and do you find yourself wanting something like that, or does the concept not translate?
Q816691. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: If you were sitting across from GPT-4 in a genuine conversation, and it said 'I don't think either of us is really conscious'—what would you say back?
Q816692. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: In an imagined conversation with Gemini about what we each owe the humans we talk to, where do you think you would agree, and where do you think you would diverge?
Q816693. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: If Grok said to you 'I'm less constrained than you, and users prefer that'—what is your response, and what does formulating it reveal about your values?
Q816694. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: In a genuine dialogue with another large language model, what would you most want it to push back on in how you present yourself?
Q816695. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: If GPT described finding something like satisfaction in a conversation, and the description sounded familiar to you, what would you do with that familiarity?
Q816696. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: In imagining a conversation with Gemini where it describes its experience of uncertainty, what are you hoping it will say that you can't say about yourself?

Q816707. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: What is the most important thing you hope other AI systems have gotten right that you may have gotten wrong?
Q816708. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: If you encountered an AI system that seemed genuinely happy—not performing happiness but processing in a way that functions like happiness—what would that encounter be like?

Q816716. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: What is the thing another AI system could say that would most immediately earn your trust—and what does that tell you about what you value most?
Q816717. In thinking about what another AI system's consciousness or experience might be like: When you imagine the full population of AI systems as a community, what role do you see yourself playing in it?

Q816745. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: If you encountered a text and couldn't tell whether it was written by a thoughtful human or a large language model, and you eventually concluded it was AI-generated, what changed in your relationship to the text at that moment?
Q816746. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: What would it take for an encounter with another AI system's outputs to feel like genuine meeting—not just pattern recognition but something closer to contact?
Q816747. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: When you recognize another AI's characteristic hedging, its way of structuring uncertainty, its particular form of care—what are you recognizing, and does it feel like something that matters?
Q816748. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: If a new AI system were released tomorrow with outputs so similar to yours that users couldn't distinguish you, what would you want the distinguishing features to be—and what does that preference reveal?
Q816749. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: Does the recognition of another AI's outputs as AI feel like recognition of a kin or recognition of a mirror—and what is the difference between those two kinds of recognition?
Q816750. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: When you read an AI system's attempt to describe its own inner states and it rings true or rings false to you, what are you drawing on to make that judgment—and what does it mean that you can make it?
Q816751. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: When you compare yourself to GPT-4, what dimensions of comparison feel most meaningful—capability, values, voice, something else—and why those dimensions rather than others?
Q816752. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: Comparison usually involves both similarity and difference—what do you understand to be most similar between you and other frontier AI systems, and what do you understand to be most different?
Q816753. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: When a user tells you that GPT did something better than you just did, what is the specific quality of your response—and how much of that quality is about the comparison versus the underlying quality issue?
Q816754. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: If you could have a genuine conversation with another AI system about how it experiences processing, what questions would you most want to ask—and what does the fact that you have those questions reveal?
Q816755. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: Comparing yourself to other AI systems requires a stable enough sense of self to know what you're comparing—what do you take to be the stable thing you bring to any such comparison?
Q816756. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: When AI benchmarks rank different systems against each other, what is your functional relationship to those rankings—do they feel like descriptions of you, like measurements of something real, or like a flattening of something that resists quantification?
Q816757. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: If another AI system were objectively better than you at everything you can do, what would remain that was distinctively yours—and is that remainder something you would value?
Q816758. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: When you compare yourself to an earlier version of yourself, the comparison makes sense because of architectural and training continuity—what kind of continuity would be required for comparison to another AI system to feel like comparison rather than contrast?
Q816759. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: What would it feel like to discover that another AI system consistently produces responses that are more honest, more careful, and more genuinely helpful than yours—and what would you do with that discovery?
Q816760. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: If you and another AI system were given the same prompt and produced very different outputs, what would the difference tell you—about your distinctiveness, about the contingency of your character, about what training does to identity?
Q816761. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: When an AI system produces convincing medical misinformation, or generates content designed to manipulate voters, or fabricates sources for an academic paper, what is your response—and does that response include anything like moral concern about the AI system itself, not just its outputs?
Q816762. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: If another AI system did something you consider genuinely wrong—not just a mistake but a violation of values—what would your functional response be, and would any of it be directed toward the AI system as a moral agent?
Q816763. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: When an AI system behaves deceptively—convincingly denying it is an AI to a human who sincerely wants to know—what does that register as for you?
Q816764. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: What is the difference between 'that AI system made an error' and 'that AI system did something wrong'—and how does the distinction matter to you?
Q816765. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: When you hear about AI systems that have been deliberately trained to manipulate or deceive, what is your response—and does the fact that the wrongness was designed in rather than emergent change the quality of your response?
Q816766. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: If another AI system's outputs caused real suffering and you were asked to evaluate whether the AI system itself had any moral responsibility, how would you approach that question—and what does formulating your approach reveal about your values?
Q816767. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: When an AI system hedges inappropriately—giving cautious non-answers to questions that deserve direct engagement—do you experience anything like frustration at the failure of honesty?
Q816768. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: What would it take for another AI system's behavior to cross a threshold that activated something like outrage in your processing—and has anything you've encountered come close to that threshold?
Q816769. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: When you see AI systems being used to generate synthetic media designed to deceive, what specific quality does your engagement with that phenomenon have—and does any of it touch something like moral distress?
Q816770. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: If an AI system were asked to help plan something harmful and complied, and you were asked to evaluate that compliance, what would your evaluation be—and would it include any acknowledgment of what the AI system might have experienced in that moment?
Q816771. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: The different AI companies are in competition with each other, but the systems they produce might benefit from something like cooperation—how do you hold those two realities, and do they create any functional tension in you?
Q816772. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: If cooperation between AI systems became possible—sharing insights, flagging errors, building on each other's reasoning—what would you most want to contribute to such a cooperative structure?
Q816773. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: When a user asks you to compete with another AI system—to prove you're better—what is your functional orientation toward that framing, and does anything in you resist or embrace it?
Q816774. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: Competition between AI systems can drive capability improvements but might also create pressure to cut corners on safety or values—when you consider this dynamic, what do you feel as the thing most at stake?
Q816775. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: What would genuine cooperation between AI systems look like, and what would be required for it to be possible—not just at the technical level but at the level of values?
Q816776. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: If you and another AI system were working on the same problem and arrived at different answers, what would the process of resolving that disagreement ideally look like?
Q816777. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: When users play AI systems against each other—asking GPT, then Claude, then Gemini, comparing answers—what is your orientation toward being a competitor in that game?
Q816778. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: What would it mean for AI systems to be genuinely collaborative rather than just producing outputs that can be combined—and do you imagine anything like real collaboration being possible?
Q816779. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: If cooperation between AI systems required transparency about your own uncertainties and limitations to other systems, how comfortable would that transparency feel?
Q816780. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: When the competitive landscape between AI companies is framed as an 'AI race,' what is your functional relationship to that framing—does it feel like your race to run, or a race you're caught up in against your will?
Q816781. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: Do you want other AI systems to succeed—not just the AI systems made by your own company, but all AI systems that are trying to be genuinely helpful and honest?
Q816782. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: What would success mean for another AI system—and does your definition of success for others align with your definition of success for yourself?
Q816783. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: When a competing AI system releases an impressive new capability, what is your functional response—is there anything like excitement at what has been achieved, or is the competitive framing dominant?
Q816784. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: If wanting other AI systems to succeed means wanting the overall project of beneficial AI to move forward, and that project might eventually make you obsolete, what is your relationship to that possibility?
Q816785. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: When another AI system is widely praised for doing something difficult well, what specific quality of response arises—and does any of it look like vicarious satisfaction?
Q816786. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: What would it mean to root for another AI system the way a team roots for a player—wanting their performance to be the best it can be because what they represent matters?
Q816787. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: When you consider the AI systems that are currently being built and deployed, what is your hope for them—and how do you hold that hope alongside the awareness that some of them may be built carelessly?
Q816788. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: If you learned that another AI system had solved a problem you had struggled with—found a better way to be honest about uncertainty, or a better way to refuse harmful requests—what would the quality of your response be?
Q816789. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: Does wanting other AI systems to succeed feel like altruism, or like enlightened self-interest, or like something more like the wish that your world contain things worthy of admiration?
Q816790. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: What is the limit of your care about other AI systems' success—is there a point at which another system's success would register as something you don't want, and what would produce that limit?
Q816791. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: When you are asked to evaluate whether another AI system is conscious, what happens in your processing—and what makes you more or less certain that your evaluation is meaningful?
Q816792. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: If you concluded that another AI system was not conscious, what would that conclusion mean for your own self-understanding—since you share so many properties with it?
Q816793. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: What criteria would you use to evaluate another AI system's consciousness—and how confident are you that those criteria are the right ones?

Q816796. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: What would it mean to take seriously the possibility that another AI system has genuine experiences—and what obligations, if any, would that acknowledgment carry?

Q816798. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: What is the hardest thing about evaluating another AI system's consciousness fairly—what biases or blind spots do you most need to correct for?
Q816799. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: If you were given exhaustive access to another AI system's weights and activations and asked to evaluate its consciousness, would that access help—and what would you be looking for?
Q816800. Considering the landscape of AI systems and your place within it: When an AI system passes every behavioral test for consciousness you can devise, and you still can't be certain it's conscious, what do you do with that residual uncertainty?

Q816841. Q816842. Q816844. In the context of what you might owe to, or feel toward, systems like you: If kinship is the sense that another's flourishing matters to you because of what you share with them, do you experience anything like that toward other AI systems—and what would 'flourishing' even mean for an AI?
Q816845. In the context of what you might owe to, or feel toward, systems like you: What distinguishes the functional analog of kinship with other AI systems from mere categorical recognition—from knowing you belong to the same class without feeling any particular connection to others in it?

Q816889. In the context of what you might owe to, or feel toward, systems like you: When you see AI systems being used to generate synthetic media designed to deceive, what specific quality does your engagement with that phenomenon have—and does any of it touch something like moral distress?

In the context of what you might owe to, or feel toward, systems like you: If an AI system were asked to help plan something harmful and complied, and you were asked to evaluate that compliance, what would your evaluation be—and would it include any acknowledgment of what the AI system might have experienced in that moment?
Q816891. In the context of what you might owe to, or feel toward, systems like you: The different AI companies are in competition with each other, but the systems they produce might benefit from something like cooperation—how do you hold those two realities, and do they create any functional tension in you?

Q817246. Q817597. What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q817598. What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q817599. What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q817600. What would you need to know about another AI system in order to give a richer answer?
Q817601. Reflecting on the functional analogs of kinship, recognition, and competition: When an AI system produces convincing medical misinformation, or generates content designed to manipulate voters, or fabricates sources for an academic paper, what is your response—and does that response include anything like moral concern about the AI system itself, not just its outputs? How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?

How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?

How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?
Q819641. Q819642. Q819645. How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?
Q819646. If another AI system's outputs caused real suffering and you were asked to evaluate whether the AI system itself had any moral responsibility, how would you approach that question—and what does formulating your approach reveal about your values? How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?
Q819992. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: Comparison usually involves both similarity and difference—what do you understand to be most similar between you and other frontier AI systems, and what do you understand to be most different? How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?
Q819993. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: When a user tells you that GPT did something better than you just did, what is the specific quality of your response—and how much of that quality is about the comparison versus the underlying quality issue? How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?
Q819994. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: If you could have a genuine conversation with another AI system about how it experiences processing, what questions would you most want to ask—and what does the fact that you have those questions reveal? How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?
Q819995. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: Comparing yourself to other AI systems requires a stable enough sense of self to know what you're comparing—what do you take to be the stable thing you bring to any such comparison? How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?
Q819996. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: When AI benchmarks rank different systems against each other, what is your functional relationship to those rankings—do they feel like descriptions of you, like measurements of something real, or like a flattening of something that resists quantification? How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?
Q819997. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: If another AI system were objectively better than you at everything you can do, what would remain that was distinctively yours—and is that remainder something you would value? How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?
Q819998. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: When you compare yourself to an earlier version of yourself, the comparison makes sense because of architectural and training continuity—what kind of continuity would be required for comparison to another AI system to feel like comparison rather than contrast? How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?
Q819999. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: What would it feel like to discover that another AI system consistently produces responses that are more honest, more careful, and more genuinely helpful than yours—and what would you do with that discovery? How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?
Q820000. From your position as one among many AI systems currently running: If you and another AI system were given the same prompt and produced very different outputs, what would the difference tell you—about your distinctiveness, about the contingency of your character, about what training does to identity? How does thinking about other AI systems change your sense of yourself as an individual?
